diagrams.title: Python Module Import Mechanism
diagrams.description: Shows how Python imports modules, the search path order, and how code is executed upon import
elements: List inside: 1. Current directory, 2. PYTHONPATH, 3. Standard library paths, 4. Site-packages
Output Arrow → Object in memory: module namespace
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete import mechanism, why PYTHONPATH matters for IoT projects, and that import statements execute module code immediately

diagrams.title: IoT Project Module Structure
diagrams.description: Hierarchical structure of a typical IoT application showing modular organization
elements: Sub-folder: sensors/ with __init__.py, temperature.py, humidity.py, pressure.py
Arrows: main.py imports from each submodule
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how to organize IoT code into logical modules - sensor drivers, communication handlers, data processors - enabling team collaboration and code reuse

diagrams.title: Module Namespace and Aliasing
diagrams.description: Demonstrates how namespaces work with modules and the alias pattern for cleaner code
elements: Box: Import statements showing all variations
Line 2: from sensor import read_temp (direct function)
Line 3: from sensor import * (all public names - not recommended)
Two overlapping circles: Global Namespace + Module Namespace
Label: dir() function lists all available names
diagrams.purpose: Students understand namespace isolation between modules, how to use different import styles, and when to use aliasing for brevity in IoT code

diagrams.title: __name__ == '__main__' Execution Flow
diagrams.description: Shows how the special __name__ variable controls code execution - critical for making modules runnable and importable
elements: True Branch → Rectangle: Run test code / main() function
False Branch → Rectangle: Module imported (skip test code)
Example Code Box: if __name__ == '__main__': print('Direct run') else: print('Imported')
Two scenarios: Running python sensor.py vs import sensor
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to create modules that can both run directly for testing and be imported by other modules without side effects - essential for IoT sensor drivers

charts.title: Built-in Modules vs External Packages vs User Modules

content.Examples: os, sys, json, time, datetime, math, random
content.IoT Use: File operations, system config, JSON data, timing
GPIO control, I2C communication, MQTT, HTTP requests

content.Syntax: from module_name import name as alias

content.Cons: Pollutes namespace, unclear what is available

charts.title: Module Selection Decision for IoT Developers
content: Step 1: Need functionality? → Check Python Standard Library first
Step 2: Not in stdlib? → Search PyPI (pip search or pypi.org)
Step 3: Found package? → pip install package-name
Step 4: Not found? → Create custom module
Step 5: Reusable across projects? → Convert to package with setup.py
Common IoT Modules: RPi.GPIO (GPIO), smbus (I2C), paho-mqtt (MQTT), requests (HTTP)

content.Purpose: Path to the module source file
Public API - names exported with 'from module import *'
Returns list of all names in module namespace

branches: Definition: Reusable .py files that encapsulate related functionality
Purpose: Code organization, namespace management, reusability across IoT projects
Types: Built-in (os, sys, json), External (RPi.GPIO, paho-mqtt), User-defined (sensor.py)
Import Mechanism: sys.path search order, module execution on import, namespace creation
Key Concepts: __name__ == '__main__', __init__.py for packages, dir() for inspection
IoT Applications: Sensor drivers (temperature, humidity), Communication (MQTT, HTTP), Data logging
Best Practices: Explicit imports, __all__ for public API, test in __main__ block, organize by function
Packages: Directories with __init__.py, hierarchical organization, import package.submodule
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting module concepts to IoT development - shows students the complete picture from basic definition to practical IoT applications
